analogy: Think of "Mass Media and Communications" through the chapter's own opening images: The mass media include a wide variety of forms, including television, newspapers, films, magazines, radio, advertisements, video games and CDs. They are referred to as ‘mass’ media because they reach mass audiences – audiences comprise very large numbers of people. They are also sometimes referred... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 7.1 The B eginnings of Modern Mass Media, 7.2 M ass Media in independenT india, Mass Media and Communications, Mass. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Mass Media and Communications is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that the mass media include a wide variety of forms, including television, newspapers, films, magazines, radio, advertisements, video games and CDs.

The text explains that they are referred to as ‘mass’ media because they reach mass audiences – audiences comprise very large numbers of people.

The text explains that they are also sometimes referred to as mass communications.

The text explains that for many in your generation, it is probably difficult to imagine a world without some form of mass media and communications.

The text explains that in many middle class households across the country people wake up only to put on the radio, switch on the television, look for the morning newspaper.

The text explains that the younger children of the same households may first glance at their mobile phones to check their missed calls.

The text explains that plumbers, electricians, carpenters, painters and sundry other service providers in many urban centres have a mobile telephone where they can be easily contacted.

The text explains that many shops in cities increasingly have a small television set.

The text explains that customers who come in may exchange bits of conversation about the cricket match being telecasted or the film being shown.

The text explains that indians abroad keep regular touch with friends and families back home over the Internet and telephone.

The text explains that migrants from working class population in the cities are regularly in touch with their families in the villages over the phone.

The text explains that have you seen the range of advertisements of mobile phones?

The text explains that laxman ¾ Imagine a world where there is no television, no cinema, no newspapers, no magazines, no internet, no telephones, no mobile phones. ¾ Write down your daily activities in a day.

The text explains that identify the occasions when you used the media in some way or the other. ¾ Find out from an older generation what life was like without any of these forms of communication.

The text explains that compare it with your life. ¾ Discuss the ways work and leisure have changed with the developments in communication technologies.

The text explains that the CBSE Board results are available on both the Internet and over the mobile phone.

The text explains that this helps us realise that like any other social institution the structure and content o f m a s s m e d i a i s shaped by changes in the economic, political and socio-cultural contexts.

The text explains that second, this help us better appreciate how the relationship between mass media and communication with society is dialectical.

The text explains that the nature and role of mass media is influenced by the society in which it is located.

The text explains that at the same time the far reaching influence of mass media on society cannot be over-emphasised.

The text explains that we shall see this dialectical relationship when we discuss in this chapter—(a) the role of media in colonial India, (b) in the first decades after independence and (c) and finally in the context of globalisation.

The text explains that you will find, therefore, that the state and/or the market have a major role in the structure and functioning of mass media.

The text explains that you will recall the concept of digital divide from the last chapter.

The text explains that although the history of print in certain societies dates back to many centuries, the first attempts at printing books using modern technologies began in Europe.

The text explains that this led the colonial government to clamp down on the nationalist press and impose censorship, for instance during the Ilbert Bill agitation in 1883.

The text explains that you will recall the general thrust of development in the early years of independence in India from your earlier chapters.

The text explains that a rational, scientific ethos was to be promoted for the building of a modern industrial society.

The text explains that the Films Division of the government produced newsreels and documentaries.

The text explains that these were shown before the screening of films in every movie theatre, documenting the development process as directed by the state. rAdio Radio broadcasting which commenced in India through amateur ‘ham’ broadcasting clubs in Kolkata and Chennai in the 1920s matured into a public broadcasting system in the 1940s during the World War II when it became a major instrument of propaganda for Allied forces in South-east Asia.

The text explains that when India gained independence in 1947, All India Radio had an infrastructure of six radio stations, located in metropolitan cities.

The text explains that after independence, the government gave priority to the expansion of the radio broadcasting infrastructure, especially in state capitals and in border areas.

The text explains that over the years, AIR has developed a formidable infrastructure for radio broadcasting in India.

The text explains that later, the Satellite Instructional Television Experiment (SITE) broadcasted directly to community viewers in the rural areas of six states between August 1975 and July 1976.

The text explains that these instructional broadcasts were broadcast to 2,400 TV sets directly for four hours daily.

The text explains that entertainment programmes grew and were directed to the urban consuming class.

The text explains that fired by the spirit of patriotic and social reforming idealism, it was able to draw in outstanding talent as the freedom struggle and movements for social change intensified and as new educational and career opportunities arose in a modernising society.

The text explains that source: Editorial ‘Yesterday, Today, Tomorrow’, The Hindu, 13 September 2003, quoted in B.P.

The text explains that at the start of the chapter we had mentioned how mass media is different from other means of communication as it requires a formal structural organisation to meet large scale capital, production and management demands.

The text explains that and also like any other social institution, the mass media also varies in structure and content according to different economic, political and socio-cultural context.

The text explains that 7.3 gloBalisaTion and The Media We have already read about the far reaching impact of globalisation as well as its close link with the communication revolution in the last chapter.

The text explains that however, until the 1970s most media companies operated within specific domestic markets in accordance with regulations from national governments.

The text explains that the growth of technology — from personal stereo systems to music television (such as the MTV) to the compact disc (CD) — have provided newer, more sophisticated ways for music to be distributed globally.

The text explains that if the Internet removes the need for all these elements by allowing music to be marketed and downloaded directly, what will be left of the music industry?

The text explains that the changes that have taken place in mass media is so immense that this chapter will probably be only able to give you a fragmentary understanding.

The text explains that as is evident, the reasons for this amazing growth in Indian language newspapers are many.

The text explains that the reason The Indian Language Newspaper Revolution The most significant happening in the last few decades has been the Indian language newspaper revolution.

The text explains that source: Audit Bureau of Circulation, 2016–17.

The text explains that these were tabloid inserts or features carrying sensational news from particular districts, as well as, classified advertisements from villages and small towns of the area.

The text explains that this process has, however, often involved cuts in prices and increasing dependence on the sponsors of advertisements who in turn have a larger say in the content of newspapers.

The text explains that while the older people read the newspaper in its entirety, younger readers often have specific interests like sports, entertainment or society gossip and directly move to the pages earmarked for these items.

The text explains that this brings us to the question whether serious political and economic issues are neglected.

The text explains that soap operas presume a history, which the regular viewer knows – he or she becomes familiar with the characters, with their personalities and their life experiences.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 7: Mass Media and Communications

revision_bullets: The mass media include a wide variety of forms, including television, newspapers, films, magazines, radio, advertisements, video games and CDs. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)
They are referred to as ‘mass’ media because they reach mass audiences – audiences comprise very large numbers of people. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)
They are also sometimes referred to as mass communications. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)
For many in your generation, it is probably difficult to imagine a world without some form of mass media and communications. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)

In many middle class households across the country people wake up only to put on the radio, switch on the television, look for the morning newspaper. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)
The younger children of the same households may first glance at their mobile phones to check their missed calls. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)

Plumbers, electricians, carpenters, painters and sundry other service providers in many urban centres have a mobile telephone where they can be easily contacted. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)
Many shops in cities increasingly have a small television set. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)
Customers who come in may exchange bits of conversation about the cricket match being telecasted or the film being shown. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)
Indians abroad keep regular touch with friends and families back home over the Internet and telephone. (NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India Ch7 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 7.1 The B eginnings of Modern Mass Media and 7.2 M ass Media in independenT india as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Mass Media and Communications; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Mass Media and Communications through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 7.1 The B eginnings of Modern Mass Media and 7.2 M ass Media in independenT india to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
